name: arjun
title: arjun
description:
HTTP parameter discovery suite
This package can find query parameters for URL endpoints.
.
Web applications use parameters (or queries) to accept user input, take the
following example into consideration.
.
http://api.example.com/v1/userinfo?id=751634589
.
This URL seems to load user information for a specific user id, but what if
there exists a parameter named admin which when set to True makes the endpoint
provide more information about the user?
This is what Arjun does, it finds valid HTTP parameters with a huge default
dictionary of 25,890 parameter names.
It takes less than 10 seconds to go through this huge list while making just
50-60 requests to the target.
.
Some features:
- Supports GET/POST/POST-JSON/POST-XML requests;
- Automatically handles rate limits and timeouts;
- Export results to: BurpSuite, text or JSON file;
- Import targets from: BurpSuite, text file or a raw request file;
- Can passively extract parameters from JS or 3 external sources.
.
Arjun is useful for penetration testing (PENTEST) and network security
analysis, serving as OSINT.

arguments.description: Chunk size. The number of parameters to be sent at once.
arguments.raw: -c  Chunk size. The number of parameters to be sent at once.

arguments.description: show this help message and exit.
arguments.raw: -h  show this help message and exit.

arguments.description: Add headers. Separate multiple headers with a new line.
arguments.raw: --headers  Add headers. Separate multiple headers with a new line.

arguments.description: show this help message and exit.
arguments.raw: --help  show this help message and exit.

arguments.description: Include this data in every request.
arguments.raw: --include  Include this data in every request.

arguments.description: Request method to use: GET/POST/XML/JSON/HEADERS. (default: GET).
arguments.raw: -m  Request method to use: GET/POST/XML/JSON/HEADERS. (default: GET).

arguments.description: Port for output to Burp Suite Proxy. Default port is 8080.
arguments.raw: -oB  Port for output to Burp Suite Proxy. Default port is 8080.

arguments.description: Collect parameter names from passive sources like wayback, commoncrawl and otx.
arguments.raw: --passive  Collect parameter names from passive sources like wayback, commoncrawl and otx.

command_examples: arjun [-h] [-u URL] [-o JSON_FILE] [-oT TEXT_FILE] [-oB [BURP_PORT]] [-d DELAY] [-t THREADS] [-w WORDLIST] [-m METHOD] [-i [IMPORT_FILE]]
use_cases: When you need Kali Linux, use arjun because HTTP parameter discovery suite.
When the engagement is in the install phase, use arjun as a cataloged Kali Linux tool with operator ACCEPT/CANCEL gating.

When you need a concrete invocation, run `arjun [-h] [-u URL] [-o JSON_FILE] [-oT TEXT_FILE] [-oB [BURP_PORT]] [-d DELAY] [-t THREADS] [-w WORDLIST] [-m METHOD] [-i [IMPORT_FILE]]` as documented upstream for arjun.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `arjun -h` as documented upstream for arjun.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `arjun -u $KFIOSA_TARGET_URL` as documented upstream for arjun.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `arjun -u $KFIOSA_TARGET_URL -o test.json` as documented upstream for arjun.
When chaining Kali Linux tools, pair arjun with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `arjun --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
